description: Lap 1 rewrites seed/article.md to fix the one bad citation: the multi-agent-coordination sentence cites '[S3]', which does not exist in sources.json, but is really supported by S2 (the retrieval-augmented paper, whose network-bottleneck framing is the actual source for that claim) — a mis-numbered reference. Correcting [S3] to [S2] makes check_claims.py exit 0 on lap 1. Demonstrates the canonical loop reaching DONE with zero API keys and zero external tools.

interactions.agent_output: Ran check_claims.py: 1 citation not found in sources.json — '[S3]', cited on the multi-agent-coordination sentence. sources.json only has S1 and S2. The sentence's claim (removing a shared network bottleneck) is actually supported by S2, the retrieval-augmented paper describing network-dependent querying; this is a mis-numbered reference, not a fabricated claim. Correcting [S3] to [S2] in the article body. Not touching sources.json or the checker.

actions.content:
# The Rise of Local-First AI Memory

Local-first agent memory systems keep state on-device instead of round-tripping
to a cloud store, cutting both latency and exposure of sensitive context [S1].

Retrieval-augmented approaches remain popular, but they depend on network
availability for every query, which local-first memory avoids by design [S2].

Some vendors claim on-device memory also improves multi-agent coordination by
removing a shared network bottleneck [S2].

Adoption is accelerating as agent frameworks add native support for local
memory backends [S1].

interactions.notes: The fix — check_claims.py now exits 0. bl run reaches DONE on lap 1, keyless, no external tool.

interactions.agent_output: Fix already applied; no further changes needed.
interactions.notes: Fallback for any lap beyond 1 — should not be reached in the happy path.
